title: Greasy Matted Fur at the Base of Your Cat's Tail: Causes & Care
meta_desc: Learn about stud tail in cats, why grease and matting occur at the tail base, and how to triage this low-urgency feline skin condition.
content_html:
What is this condition?

The oily, matted fur at the base of your cat's tail is often caused by a condition known as 'stud tail' or supracaudal gland hyperplasia. Cats have specialized sebaceous glands on the top of their tail that produce natural oils. When these glands become overactive, the excess sebum leads to a greasy appearance, crusting, and eventually thick matting of the fur. While common in intact males, it can occur in any cat regardless of gender or neuter status.

Is it an emergency?

Urgency Level: Low. In most cases, stud tail is a cosmetic issue or a mild skin irritation and is not a medical emergency. However, it should not be ignored. If the area becomes red, swollen, develops a foul odor, or if you see pus, your cat may have a secondary bacterial infection that requires a non-emergency veterinary appointment.

Why a photo helps triage

Providing a clear, well-lit photo of the affected area is incredibly helpful for triage. An image allows a veterinary professional to see the skin beneath the fur to check for inflammation, sores, or signs of infection. This helps determine whether the condition can be managed with at-home grooming or if medical intervention is necessary to prevent the condition from worsening.

faqs.question: Can I use human shampoo to clean the grease?
faqs.answer: It is best to avoid human shampoos as they can disrupt a cat's skin pH. Your vet may recommend a specific degreasing shampoo containing benzoyl peroxide or salicylic acid.

faqs.question: Will neutering my cat help with this condition?
faqs.answer: If the cat is an intact male, neutering can often reduce the hormonal drive that causes the glands to overproduce oil, though it may not resolve the issue entirely.

faqs.question: Should I try to cut the mats out myself?
faqs.answer: Use extreme caution. Cat skin is very thin and easily nicked. If the mats are tight against the skin, it is safer to have a professional groomer or vet technician remove them with electric clippers.
